title: Tiny Particles Collide in a Big Way
story: Zoom! In 1981, scientists at CERN made tiny particles crash into each other at super-fast speeds. On July 10, they smashed protons and antiprotons together for the first time ever. These particles are so small you can't see them, but when they collide, they create a burst of energy more powerful than you can imagine. It's like a microscopic fireworks show that helps us understand how the universe works!

title: Flying High: A Trapeze Triumph
story: Soaring through the air, Miguel Vasquez amazed the crowd with a breathtaking feat. On July 10, 1982, this daring acrobat became the first person to successfully perform a quadruple somersault on a trapeze in public. title: Juno's Close Encounter with Jupiter's Storm
story: Swooping through space, NASA's Juno spacecraft zoomed past Jupiter's famous Great Red Spot in 2017. This massive storm has swirled for centuries, and Juno got an incredible view! title: Death Valley's Sizzling Record

title: Telstar: The Sky-High Phone Booth

title: Ancient Skull Rewrites Human History
story: A groundbreaking discovery shook the world of archaeology in 2019. Scientists found a 210,000-year-old skull in Apidima Cave, southern Greece. Imagine trying to stay cool without air conditioning. People probably flocked to lakes and rivers, desperately seeking relief from the intense heat.
activity_question: What was the record-breaking temperature in Maine?

title: Uruguay Joins the Book Club
story: Bookworms and artists in Uruguay had reason to celebrate in 1967. On July 10 that year, the country became part of a special group called the Berne Convention. This wasn't just any club - it was a worldwide agreement to protect the rights of authors, musicians, and other creative people. Uruguay's move meant local writers and artists could now share their work with the world, knowing it would be safe from copycats!

follow_up_question: Why is it important to protect the rights of creative people?

title: Watering Fields: A Farmer's Bright Idea
story: Frank Zybach had a thirst for innovation. Born in Lafayette, Oregon on July 10, 1894, this American farmer would change agriculture forever. Zybach invented center-pivot irrigation, a clever system that waters crops in a circular pattern. His creation became the most widely used irrigation method in the United States, helping farmers grow more food with less work.

follow_up_question: How does center-pivot irrigation water crops?

title: French Allies Join the American Revolution
story: Bonjour, America! In 1780, French soldiers sailed across the ocean to help in the fight for independence. title: Jazz Guitar Legend's Fiery Debut

This performance marked the birth of 'gypsy jazz', a genre that would influence musicians for generations to come.

bonus_fact: Django Reinhardt played guitar brilliantly despite having two fingers paralyzed from a fire accident.

title: Maryland's Hottest Day Ever
story: Whew! It was sizzling in Maryland on July 10, 1936. Cumberland and Frederick felt like frying pans as temperatures soared to a scorching 109°F (43°C). This wasn't just hot; it was the hottest temperature ever recorded in Maryland's history! People probably spent the day seeking shade, gulping ice water, and dreaming of cooler days.
activity_question: What was the record-breaking temperature in Maryland on this day?
follow_up_question: How might you stay cool on an extremely hot day like this one?

title: New Jersey Sizzles in Record Heat
story: Whew! It was a scorcher in Runyon, New Jersey on July 10, 1936. The temperature soared to a sizzling 110°F (43°C), setting the all-time heat record for the state. People sought relief any way they could, from splashing in fountains to enjoying ice-cold lemonade. This extreme weather reminds us to stay cool and hydrated during hot summer days.
activity_question: What was the record-breaking temperature in Runyon, New Jersey?
follow_up_question: What are some ways you can stay cool on very hot days?

title: West Virginia's Hottest Day Ever
story: Whew! It was sizzling in Martinsburg, West Virginia on July 10, 1936. The temperature soared to a scorching 112°F (44°C), setting the all-time heat record for the state. People probably sought shade, gulped cold drinks, and dreamed of swimming pools. This extreme heat was part of the infamous Dust Bowl era, when many parts of America faced severe drought and heat waves.
activity_question: What was the record-breaking temperature in Martinsburg, West Virginia?
follow_up_question: How might people have stayed cool on such a hot day without modern air conditioning?

title: Parking Meters Arrive in England
story: Ding! The sound of coins dropping into metal slots echoed through English streets in 1958. On July 10, the first parking meters appeared in London. Hundreds of these shiny new machines lined the curbs, ready to keep track of how long cars stayed parked. It was a big change for drivers, who now had to remember to bring coins and watch the clock!
activity_question: What sound did the story mention when describing the new parking meters?
bonus_fact: Parking meters were invented to help shops by encouraging drivers to move their cars more often.

title: Hurricane Dennis Strikes Florida
story: Winds howled and waves crashed as Hurricane Dennis roared ashore on July 10, 2005. The powerful storm slammed into Florida's Panhandle, unleashing its fury on coastal communities. Homes shook, trees toppled, and streets flooded as Dennis carved a path of destruction. Despite the chaos, brave emergency workers rushed to help those in need.
activity_question: Where did Hurricane Dennis make landfall?
follow_up_question: How can people prepare for hurricanes to stay safe?
